title: Lesson 329
transcript: Threat modeling is the systematic identification, enumeration, and prioritization of threats related to an asset. In other words, these are the methods that we use to somehow figure out what are the threats that are applicable to a system. And the reason we have threat models is that it can sometimes be a bit of an intractable problem to try and figure out what are all the threats that are applicable to a system, especially our modern systems that are so unbelievably complex. Trying to think through and figure out what are all the possible threats that we might have to think about related to this asset can be a daunting task. Threat models are a way to help us systematically think through this. And there's a lot of different threat models out there, two major ones you need to know about, stride and pasta. Stride was created by Microsoft, and the first letter of each letter in Stride is basically what this whole methodology is about. So the S stands for spoofing. Spoofing is where an attacker pretends to be somewhere else, someone else, usually to get unauthorized access. And so what spoofing is all about is it's a violation of authentication. The T stands for tampering. And this is where an attacker tries to modify the data at rest or in transit to try and change the state of the system to maybe to give himself elevator privileges, whatever. But tampering is a violation of integrity. Next, repudiation. This is where an attacker is somehow able to do something on the system and we can't attribute it to them. We don't know that they did it. So it's a violation of non-repudiation. The I, and this is kind of where the acronym starts to fall apart, the I stands for information disclosure. And this is where the attacker is somehow, the threat here, is where the attacker is able to get information out of a system that they should not be able to. And so information disclosure is a violation of confidentiality. The D stands for denial of service. And denial of service is simply when an authorized user isn't able to get access to some system, some resource, for whatever reason. There's lots of different ways to cause a denial of service attack. And so denial of services ultimately is a violation of availability. And the E stands for elevation of privilege. This is ultimately what any attacker wants to do on a system. They want to figure out how do they go from, you know, just regular user level access to root or administrative access. Because with elevated privileges, it gives you a lot more control of the system, gives you the ability to cover your tracks. And so elevation of privileges isn't a violation of authorization. And that's the STRIDE methodology. STRIDE's a good methodology, but there's a better one that you can use. It takes a lot more time, it's a lot more involved, but it'll give you much more thorough results. And that method is... PASTA, which stands for the Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis. The big difference with PASTA is that it is risk centric. It looks at all the possible, it takes risk into consideration when you're conducting a threat analysis. And it takes into, for instance, it takes into account just how critical, how valuable, what business purpose does a system provide? Because that makes a big difference to what the threats are that are applicable to a system. And so it looks both at the business and the technical aspects of a system. It's a much more thorough analysis. It takes a lot longer, but it's more thorough. Here are the six major steps involved, or sorry, the seven major steps involved in PASTA. You don't have to memorize these, but it gives you an idea of how it's a much more thorough and sort of risk-based analysis. You start with defining objectives, and then you define the scope, the application decomposition, then you do the threat analysis, the vulnerability and weakness analysis, the attack modeling, and then the risk and impact analysis. It's a long and arduous process, so produces some very thorough and useful results. And so if you get a question asking you, what's the difference between Stride or Pasta and which would you use in which situation? If you want to do a quick, simpler analysis, use Stride. If you want to do a much more, especially risk-based or an analysis that takes into account business perspective of a system, use Pasta. We'll be right back.
